body_systems.name: Heart
body_systems.description: The muscular organ at the center of the circulatory system, pumping oxygenated blood throughout the body. In traditional medicine, the heart is also the seat of consciousness, joy, and spirit (Shen in TCM). Its health reflects both cardiovascular function and emotional openness.

body_systems.name: Liver
body_systems.description: The body's primary detoxification organ, responsible for filtering blood, metabolizing hormones and toxins, producing bile, and storing glycogen. In TCM the liver governs the smooth flow of Qi throughout the body and is associated with vision, planning, and the emotion of anger.

body_systems.name: Kidneys
body_systems.description: The paired organs that filter blood, regulate fluid balance, maintain electrolyte equilibrium, and eliminate metabolic waste as urine. In TCM the kidneys store Jing (essence), govern birth, growth, reproduction, and aging, and are the root of all yin and yang in the body.

body_systems.name: Lungs
body_systems.description: The paired respiratory organs responsible for gas exchange, bringing oxygen into the blood and releasing carbon dioxide. In TCM the lungs govern Qi and respiration, control the skin and body hair, and are associated with the emotion of grief. They are the body's interface with the atmosphere.

body_systems.name: Brain
body_systems.description: The central organ of the nervous system, governing cognition, perception, memory, motor control, and consciousness itself. The brain consumes roughly 20% of the body's energy and is exquisitely sensitive to nutritional status, blood flow, and toxic exposure.

body_systems.name: Stomach
body_systems.description: The muscular organ that receives food from the esophagus, secretes hydrochloric acid and digestive enzymes, and begins the process of protein breakdown. In TCM the stomach is the origin of post-natal Qi and its descending function is essential for proper digestion.

body_systems.name: Spleen
body_systems.description: An organ of the lymphatic system involved in blood filtration, immune surveillance, and recycling of old red blood cells. In TCM the spleen is the central organ of digestion and transformation, governing the extraction of nutrients from food and the production of blood and Qi.

body_systems.name: Skin
body_systems.description: The body's largest organ, forming the protective boundary between the internal environment and the external world. The skin regulates temperature, provides immune defense, synthesizes vitamin D, and serves as a mirror of internal health. Skin conditions frequently reflect liver, digestive, or hormonal imbalances.

body_systems.name: Bones
body_systems.description: The skeletal framework providing structural support, protection of vital organs, mineral storage, and blood cell production in the marrow. Bone health depends on adequate minerals, hormonal balance, weight-bearing activity, and vitamin D status.

body_systems.name: Blood
body_systems.description: The liquid connective tissue circulating through the cardiovascular system, carrying oxygen, nutrients, hormones, and immune cells to every cell while removing carbon dioxide and metabolic waste. Blood quality and volume are central to vitality in every traditional medical system.

body_systems.name: Reproductive System
body_systems.description: The organs and glands responsible for fertility, sexual function, and the continuation of the species. Includes the uterus, ovaries, testes, and associated hormonal axis. Reproductive health is intimately tied to overall vitality, hormonal balance, and the creative life force.

body_systems.name: Digestive Tract
body_systems.description: The continuous tube from mouth to anus responsible for mechanical and chemical breakdown of food, absorption of nutrients, and elimination of waste. Includes the esophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, and associated organs. The gut is now recognized as a second brain with its own enteric nervous system.

body_systems.name: Nervous System
body_systems.description: The body's master communication network, comprising the brain, spinal cord, and peripheral nerves. Governs sensation, movement, thought, emotion, and the regulation of every organ system through electrical and chemical signaling.

body_systems.name: Immune System
body_systems.description: The complex network of organs, cells, and molecules that defends the body against pathogens, abnormal cells, and foreign substances. Includes the thymus, bone marrow, spleen, lymph nodes, and circulating white blood cells. Immune health is the foundation of resistance to disease.

body_systems.name: Endocrine System
body_systems.description: The network of glands that produce, store, and secrete hormones directly into the bloodstream, regulating metabolism, growth, reproduction, mood, and virtually every physiological process. Includes the pituitary, thyroid, adrenals, pancreas, and gonads.

body_systems.name: Lymphatic System
body_systems.description: The network of vessels, nodes, and organs that transports lymph fluid, filters pathogens and cellular debris, and returns interstitial fluid to the bloodstream. The lymphatic system is essential for immune function and fluid balance and has no pump of its own, relying on muscular movement and breathing.

body_systems.name: Adrenal Glands
body_systems.description: Small glands atop each kidney that produce cortisol, adrenaline, aldosterone, and DHEA. The adrenals govern the body's stress response, energy regulation, blood pressure, and mineral balance. Chronic stress depletes adrenal reserves, leading to fatigue and hormonal cascade failures.

body_systems.name: Thyroid
body_systems.description: A butterfly-shaped gland in the neck that produces hormones (T3 and T4) controlling metabolism, body temperature, heart rate, and energy production. Thyroid disorders are among the most common endocrine conditions, manifesting as either hyperactivity or sluggishness of metabolic function.

body_systems.name: Pineal Gland
body_systems.description: A small pine-cone shaped endocrine gland in the brain that produces melatonin, regulating circadian rhythm and sleep-wake cycles. Across esoteric traditions the pineal is considered the seat of the third eye, governing intuition, visionary perception, and connection to higher consciousness.

body_systems.name: Pancreas
body_systems.description: A dual-function organ that produces digestive enzymes (exocrine function) and hormones including insulin and glucagon (endocrine function) that regulate blood sugar levels. Pancreatic health is central to metabolic stability and diabetes prevention.

body_systems.name: Gallbladder
body_systems.description: A small pear-shaped organ beneath the liver that stores and concentrates bile produced by the liver, releasing it into the small intestine to aid fat digestion. In TCM the gallbladder is paired with the liver and governs decision-making, courage, and judgment.

body_systems.name: Bladder
body_systems.description: A hollow muscular organ that stores urine received from the kidneys via the ureters and releases it through the urethra. The bladder's function is closely tied to kidney health and water metabolism. In TCM the bladder meridian is the longest in the body, running along the entire spine.

body_systems.name: Muscles
body_systems.description: The contractile tissues that enable movement, maintain posture, generate heat, and support skeletal structure. Muscle health depends on adequate protein, minerals (especially magnesium, potassium, calcium), proper nerve signaling, and sufficient blood flow.

body_systems.name: Eyes
body_systems.description: The sensory organs of vision, containing specialized photoreceptor cells that convert light into neural signals. Eye health is intimately connected to liver function in TCM, cardiovascular health (retinal blood supply), and antioxidant status. The eyes are considered extensions of the brain.

body_systems.name: Oral Cavity
body_systems.description: The mouth, teeth, gums, and tongue, forming the gateway to the digestive system. Oral health is a critical indicator of systemic health, with periodontal disease linked to cardiovascular disease, diabetes, and chronic inflammation throughout the body.

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: A walnut half resembles the two hemispheres of the brain, complete with wrinkled folds mimicking cerebral convolutions. Even the shell resembles the skull.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Rich in omega-3 fatty acids (ALA), polyphenols, and vitamin E that support neuronal membrane integrity, reduce neuroinflammation, and improve cognitive function
plant_part_correspondences.notes: One of the most celebrated examples of the Doctrine of Signatures across cultures

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: The kidney bean is shaped exactly like a human kidney, including the hilum that mirrors the renal hilum where blood vessels enter and exit.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Provides plant-based protein, molybdenum, folate, and iron. Supports kidney function through gentle diuretic action and mineral replenishment
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Named specifically for its resemblance to the kidney organ

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: When sliced, a tomato reveals four chambers that mirror the four chambers of the human heart. Its red color echoes the color of blood and cardiac tissue.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Rich in lycopene, a potent antioxidant that reduces LDL oxidation, lowers blood pressure, and protects against cardiovascular disease
plant_part_correspondences.notes: The red color is produced by lycopene, the very compound most protective to the heart

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: Celery stalks resemble long bones. The plant is roughly 23% sodium, and bones are also roughly 23% sodium, an unusual nutritional parallel.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Provides silicon, vitamin K, and organic sodium that support bone mineralization and joint lubrication. Anti-inflammatory polyacetylenes reduce joint swelling
plant_part_correspondences.notes: The sodium content mirrors the mineral composition of bone tissue

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: The gnarly shape of ginger root resembles the stomach organ. Its warming, pungent quality signals its action on gastric function.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Gingerols and shogaols stimulate gastric motility, relieve nausea, reduce gastric inflammation, and support digestive enzyme secretion
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Ginger has extensive clinical evidence for treating nausea and gastroparesis

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: A cross-section of a carrot reveals a pattern resembling the human iris and pupil, with radiating lines similar to the iris pattern.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Rich in beta-carotene (converted to vitamin A/retinol), essential for rhodopsin production in rod cells, night vision, and corneal health
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Vitamin A deficiency remains the leading cause of preventable blindness worldwide

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: A cluster of grapes hanging on the vine resembles the alveolar clusters in the lungs, the tiny air sacs where gas exchange occurs.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Resveratrol and proanthocyanidins reduce pulmonary inflammation, thin mucus, and protect alveolar tissue from oxidative damage
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Grape seed extract has clinical evidence for respiratory protection

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: The sweet potato resembles the elongated shape of the pancreas and has a naturally sweet flavor connected to blood sugar regulation.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Despite its sweetness, sweet potato has a low glycemic index and contains caiapo, a compound shown to improve insulin sensitivity and lower fasting blood glucose
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Traditional use in multiple cultures for blood sugar support

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: Beets are deep crimson-red, the color of blood. When sliced, they bleed red juice that stains like blood.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Rich in dietary nitrates (converted to nitric oxide for vasodilation), iron, folate, and betaine. Supports red blood cell production, blood oxygenation, and cardiovascular function
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Beet juice is clinically shown to lower blood pressure and improve athletic endurance through enhanced blood flow

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: The avocado resembles the shape of the uterus and cervix. It takes exactly 9 months for an avocado to grow from blossom to ripened fruit.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Rich in folate (essential for fetal neural tube development), healthy monounsaturated fats, vitamin E, and potassium that support hormonal balance and reproductive health
plant_part_correspondences.notes: The word avocado derives from the Aztec word ahuacatl, meaning testicle, reflecting its traditional association with fertility

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: Figs hang in pairs on the tree and when opened reveal a seed-filled interior resembling the internal structure of the testes or ovaries.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Rich in zinc, magnesium, and B vitamins that support gonadal function. Contains ficin enzymes and polyphenols that modulate reproductive hormones
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Figs have been associated with fertility across Mediterranean, Middle Eastern, and Indian traditions for millennia

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: The artichoke's layered, protective structure mirrors the liver's lobular architecture.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Contains cynarin and silymarin-like compounds that stimulate bile production, protect hepatocytes from toxic damage, and lower LDL cholesterol
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Artichoke leaf extract has strong clinical evidence as a hepatoprotective agent

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: The bright yellow color and bitter-sour taste signal its action on the hepatobiliary system.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Citric acid and d-limonene stimulate bile production and liver detoxification enzymes. Vitamin C acts as a primary antioxidant in liver tissue
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Warm lemon water is a widespread folk remedy for morning liver support

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: Watermelon's high water content and its role as a natural diuretic signal its affinity for the urinary system.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Contains citrulline (converted to arginine for nitric oxide production), potassium, and abundant water that supports kidney flushing, reduces uric acid, and prevents stone formation
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Watermelon seed tea is a traditional kidney tonic in many cultures

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: Small, hard seeds that must be cracked open to reveal their nutrients, like accessing the marrow within bone.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Extremely rich in calcium, magnesium, zinc, and phosphorus, the primary minerals of bone tissue. Contains sesamin, which may inhibit osteoclast activity
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Sesame is one of the highest plant sources of calcium, critical for bone density

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: The cucumber's smooth, hydrated skin mirrors healthy human skin. Its high water and silica content signal its dermatological affinity.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Contains silica (essential for collagen synthesis), vitamin C, caffeic acid, and abundant water that hydrate skin from within. Topically cooling and anti-inflammatory
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Topical cucumber application for skin care dates back thousands of years

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: The deep red, juice-filled seeds resemble blood cells contained within chambers, echoing the heart's chambered structure.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Punicalagins and punicic acid reduce arterial plaque formation, lower blood pressure, and decrease LDL oxidation. Among the most potent cardiovascular-protective foods known
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Clinical trials demonstrate pomegranate juice reverses carotid artery thickness

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: When sliced open, papaya reveals a hollow interior resembling the GI tract, with seeds clustered like intestinal villi.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Contains papain, a proteolytic enzyme that aids protein digestion, reduces bloating, and soothes intestinal inflammation. Rich in fiber for peristaltic support
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Papain is used therapeutically as a digestive enzyme supplement

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: Mushrooms grow from decaying matter, transforming death into life, mirroring the immune system's role in destroying pathogens and renewing the body.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Beta-glucan polysaccharides activate macrophages, natural killer cells, and dendritic cells. Lentinan (shiitake) and grifolan (maitake) are potent immunomodulators
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Medicinal mushrooms have millennia of use in TCM and increasingly strong clinical evidence

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: The deep blue-purple color indicates high anthocyanin content, compounds with particular affinity for crossing the blood-brain barrier.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Anthocyanins improve hippocampal neuroplasticity, enhance memory consolidation, reduce neuroinflammation, and protect against age-related cognitive decline
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Clinical trials show regular blueberry consumption delays brain aging by up to 2.5 years

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: The curved shape of a banana suggests muscle fibers, and its use by athletes reflects its muscular affinity.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Rich in potassium, magnesium, and natural sugars that prevent muscle cramps, support electrolyte balance, and provide quick energy for muscular contraction
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Potassium is essential for muscle depolarization and contraction cycles

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: Brazil nuts grow in a pod-like shell cluster resembling a thyroid gland, and their selenium content directly serves thyroid function.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: The single richest food source of selenium, essential for the deiodinase enzymes that convert T4 to active T3. One nut provides the full daily selenium requirement
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Selenium deficiency is a major cause of impaired thyroid hormone conversion

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: Olives resemble small lymph nodes, and olive oil has long been used to support lymphatic drainage through massage.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Oleocanthal and hydroxytyrosol have potent anti-inflammatory properties that reduce lymphatic congestion. Oleic acid supports the fluidity of lymphatic vessel membranes
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Olive oil is the primary fat of the Mediterranean diet, associated with reduced chronic inflammation

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: The deep green color indicates high magnesium content (central atom of chlorophyll), the most critical mineral for adrenal function.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Provide magnesium, B vitamins, vitamin C, and iron, all of which are rapidly depleted during chronic stress and essential for adrenal hormone synthesis
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Vitamin C is found in higher concentration in the adrenal glands than almost any other organ

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: The deep red-purple color and small round shape of tart cherries mirror the tiny pineal gland, and they are one of the few natural food sources of melatonin.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Contains bioavailable melatonin that directly supports pineal function, regulates circadian rhythm, and improves sleep quality. Also rich in anthocyanins with neuroprotective properties
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Clinical trials demonstrate tart cherry juice improves sleep duration and quality in older adults

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: The pungent, bitter taste of radish signals its cholagogue action, stimulating bile flow from the gallbladder.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Contains glucosinolates and isothiocyanates that stimulate bile production, promote gallbladder contraction, and help prevent gallstone formation
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Black radish is used medicinally throughout Europe as a specific gallbladder tonic

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: The small, nail-shaped dried flower bud has been placed directly on sore teeth for centuries, its shape fitting into tooth sockets.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Eugenol is a potent local anesthetic and antimicrobial. Clove oil numbs dental pain on contact and kills oral pathogens including those causing gingivitis and periodontitis
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Clove oil remains an ingredient in modern dental preparations and was among the first local anesthetics used in dentistry

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: This Andean root grows at extreme altitude where endocrine function must adapt to harsh conditions, signaling its adaptogenic endocrine affinity.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Macamides and macaenes modulate the hypothalamic-pituitary axis, supporting balanced hormone production without containing hormones themselves. Improves energy, libido, and fertility
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Maca is one of the few plants with clinical evidence for broadly supporting endocrine balance across both sexes

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: The orange color indicates beta-carotene, and its grounding, earthy nature aligns with the TCM Earth element governing the spleen.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Rich in beta-carotene, iron, and zinc, all supporting immune cell production in the spleen. The gentle, nourishing quality supports spleen qi in TCM dietary therapy
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Sweet, warming foods are considered spleen-nourishing in TCM dietary therapy

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: Flowers, as the heart of the plant and the organ of reproduction and attraction, correspond to the human heart. Rose and hawthorn flowers are premier heart remedies.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Flower medicines tend to work on the emotional and energetic heart as well as the physical organ, opening grief, restoring joy, and regulating cardiac rhythm
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Hawthorn flower is the most widely used cardiac herb in European phytotherapy

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: Seeds contain the concentrated intelligence and DNA blueprint of the entire plant. Nuts and seeds, especially walnuts, resemble the brain and feed it.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Seeds and nuts provide essential fatty acids, phospholipids, and trace minerals critical for neuronal membrane structure and neurotransmitter synthesis
plant_part_correspondences.notes: The concentrated genetic information in a seed parallels the brain as the body's information center

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: Roots grow underground in contact with water and minerals, echoing the kidneys' role in filtering and mineral regulation. Root medicines often have diuretic or tonifying kidney action.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Root medicines like dandelion root, marshmallow root, and nettle root support kidney filtration, fluid balance, and mineral retention
plant_part_correspondences.notes: The root's underground, water-extracting function mirrors kidney filtration

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: Leaves are the lungs of the plant, performing gas exchange (photosynthesis). Lung-shaped leaves like mullein especially indicate pulmonary affinity.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Leaf medicines for the lungs include mullein, coltsfoot, lungwort, and eucalyptus, which open airways, thin mucus, and support respiration
plant_part_correspondences.notes: The stomata of leaves exchange CO2 and O2, just as alveoli exchange gases in human lungs

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: Deep taproots that penetrate dense earth mirror the liver's deep work of detoxification and transformation. Many liver herbs are bitter roots.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Bitter root medicines like dandelion, burdock, yellow dock, and Oregon grape root stimulate bile production, support hepatic detoxification enzymes, and clear liver congestion
plant_part_correspondences.notes: The bitterness of these roots activates hepatobiliary function through bitter receptor stimulation

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: Roots are the digestive system of the plant, absorbing nutrients from the soil. Aromatic roots like ginger directly support gastric function.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Root medicines provide carminative, digestive-stimulating, and anti-nausea actions. Ginger root, turmeric root, and calamus root are premier digestive remedies
plant_part_correspondences.notes: The root absorbs and transforms nutrients from soil just as the gut absorbs and transforms food

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: Bark is the skeleton and structural support of the tree, corresponding to the skeletal system. Hard, mineralizing barks correspond to bone.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Bark medicines like white willow, cramp bark, and oak bark provide anti-inflammatory, astringent, and mineralizing actions that support skeletal integrity
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Bark provides structural rigidity to trees just as bone provides structural rigidity to the body

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: Bark is the skin of the tree, its outermost protective layer. This direct correspondence makes bark medicines potent for skin conditions.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Bark medicines like slippery elm, witch hazel, and white oak provide astringent, wound-healing, and protective actions for skin conditions
plant_part_correspondences.notes: The bark protects the tree from the external environment just as skin protects the body

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: Resin and sap are the blood of the plant, flowing through vascular tissue to nourish and protect. Red resins especially correspond to blood.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Resin and sap medicines like dragon's blood, myrrh, and pine resin have blood-moving, wound-sealing, and antimicrobial actions that directly correspond to blood function
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Sap flows through plant vasculature just as blood flows through human vasculature

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: Flowers are the reproductive organs of the plant, containing stamens and pistils. This direct homology makes flower medicines potent for reproductive issues.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Flower medicines like red clover blossom, chamomile, and jasmine support hormonal balance, fertility, and reproductive organ health
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Pollen, ovules, and the act of pollination directly mirror human reproductive processes

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: Seeds are the product of plant reproduction, carrying the genetic blueprint for new life. Fruits protect and nourish seeds, echoing the womb.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Seed medicines like vitex berry, saw palmetto berry, and raspberry provide hormonal modulation and reproductive tonic actions
plant_part_correspondences.notes: The seed-to-fruit relationship mirrors the egg-to-womb relationship in human reproduction

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: Flowers represent the plant's highest expression and most refined energies, corresponding to the subtle electrical nature of the nervous system.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Flower nervines like lavender, chamomile, passionflower, and linden calm nervous system excitability, promote GABA activity, and restore parasympathetic tone
plant_part_correspondences.notes: The delicate, aromatic nature of flowers resonates with the refined signaling of the nervous system

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: Fungi inhabit the boundary between life and decay, mirroring the immune system's role as the body's boundary defense and regeneration force.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Medicinal fungi like reishi, chaga, turkey tail, and lion's mane contain beta-glucans and triterpenes that modulate immune cell activity and cytokine production
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Mycelial networks in soil echo the distributed network architecture of the immune system

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: The stem provides muscular support and locomotion-like flexibility to the plant, bending without breaking. Fibrous stems correspond to muscular tissue.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Stem and stalk medicines provide anti-spasmodic, relaxant, and strengthening actions for muscular tissue
plant_part_correspondences.notes: The vascular bundles in stems resemble the parallel fiber arrangement of skeletal muscle

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: Bright, open, eye-like flowers like eyebright (Euphrasia) visually resemble the eye, with patterns mimicking the iris and pupil.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Eyebright, calendula, and chamomile flowers provide anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and astringent actions that soothe and protect ocular tissue
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Eyebright (Euphrasia officinalis) is named for its specific visual resemblance to the eye

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: Underground roots absorb and process nutrients from the earth, mirroring the stomach's role in receiving and breaking down food.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Warming aromatic roots like ginger, galangal, and calamus stimulate gastric acid, improve motility, and reduce nausea through direct gastric action
plant_part_correspondences.notes: The root is the first point of nutrient extraction in the plant, just as the stomach is in the body

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: Cranberries grow in bogs (water-rich environments), and their tart, astringent quality signals affinity for the urinary tract.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Proanthocyanidins prevent bacterial adhesion (especially E. coli) to the bladder wall, reducing UTI recurrence. Urinary acidification creates a hostile environment for pathogens
plant_part_correspondences.notes: One of the best-studied foods for urinary tract health, with strong clinical evidence

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: The intensely bitter taste and bumpy, irregular surface signal its action on metabolic organs. Used across Asia, Africa, and the Caribbean for blood sugar regulation.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Contains charantin, vicine, and polypeptide-p, compounds that mimic insulin action, increase glucose uptake, and improve pancreatic beta-cell function
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Bitter melon is used traditionally as a food medicine for diabetes in Ayurvedic, Chinese, and African healing systems

plant_part_correspondences.signature_description: Red dates (jujube) are a foundational blood-building and spleen-nourishing food in TCM, their sweet taste and warm nature directly tonifying spleen qi.
plant_part_correspondences.therapeutic_action: Rich in iron, B vitamins, and natural sugars that support blood production and spleen qi. Cyclic AMP in jujubes supports immune cell proliferation in the spleen
plant_part_correspondences.notes: Red dates appear in more TCM formulas than almost any other food-herb, specifically for spleen qi support
